# Developing Ideas

Developing ideas have enough structure to discuss with collaborators or students, but they still need refinement before becoming active projects.

## Reading-based intervention mechanisms

Book interventions may work through multiple pathways: narrative transportation, self-reflection, new behavioral scripts, perceived social norms, or identity change. A useful next step is to compare these mechanisms in a single study.

## Common ingroup identity and polarization

Identity-based interventions may reduce hostility toward political outgroups when they make a shared identity salient, meaningful, and action-guiding. The challenge is to identify identities that feel authentic rather than imposed.

## AI-supported study materials

AI can help generate candidate messages, vignettes, or survey items, but those materials still need human review, theoretical grounding, and empirical validation.

## Effortless prosociality and cognitive habits

Prosocial behavior may become more reliable when it is supported by internalized values and automatic cognitive habits rather than only effortful self-control. A useful research program would test whether compassion, loving-kindness, or moral training changes behavior especially under low-control conditions.

## Dreams as low-control observation

Dreams may offer an exploratory window into automatic tendencies when waking self-control is reduced. This idea is not about blaming people for dream content, but about whether changes in dream behavior could help track internalization or moral habit formation.

## Dream behavior and waking behavior

Dream diaries could be used to test whether behavior in dream situations corresponds to waking behavior, implicit attitudes, or behavior under low-control conditions. A first mature version would focus on aggression or prosocial behavior because those domains have existing implicit, explicit, other-report, and behavioral measures.
